diagrams.name: coldstar-architecture
diagrams.description: Coldstar is an air-gapped Solana cold wallet system composed of four repositories. coldstar-core is a Rust offline transaction signer that runs on a permanently air-gapped device with no network access, holding private keys in secure enclave storage. coldstar-app is a React Native mobile application that serves as the user-facing wallet interface, displaying balances, constructing unsigned transactions, and broadcasting signed transactions to Solana RPC nodes. coldstar-bridge is a QR-based transaction relay that encodes unsigned transactions as animated QR codes on the mobile device, which the air-gapped device scans via camera, signs locally with the Rust signer, and then displays the signed transaction as a new QR code for the mobile app to scan back. coldstar-docs contains protocol specifications and security documentation. The complete transaction flow is: User initiates transfer on coldstar-app, the app constructs an unsigned Solana transaction, encodes it as a QR code, the air-gapped device running coldstar-core scans the QR, deserializes the transaction, signs it with the ed25519 private key via the Rust signer, renders the signed transaction as a return QR code, the mobile app scans the signed QR, deserializes it, and broadcasts it to a Solana RPC endpoint. No private key material ever touches a networked device, achieving true air-gap security.

diagrams.name: northcatch-architecture
diagrams.description: NorthCatch is a mobile fishing app built with Expo and React Native, targeting both iOS and Android via EAS Build and distributed through TestFlight and eventually the App Store under bundle ID app.replit.northcatch. The frontend uses Expo Router for file-based navigation across screens including catch logging, social feed, profile, and map views. Catch data is stored locally in SQLite for full offline functionality, allowing anglers to log catches without cell service. Each catch record includes species, weight, length, GPS coordinates, timestamp, weather conditions, and one or more photos captured via the device camera. Photos are uploaded to cloud storage (S3 or Cloudflare R2) with optimized thumbnails generated on upload. A cloud sync API reconciles local SQLite records with the remote database when connectivity is restored, using last-write-wins conflict resolution. The social feed aggregates catches from followed users, displaying catch cards with photos, species identification results, location maps, and weather context. Species identification uses an image classification model that suggests fish species from catch photos. Weather integration pulls conditions at the GPS coordinates and timestamp of each catch from a third-party weather API.

diagrams.name: ace-architecture
diagrams.description: ACE (AI Content Engine) is a content ingestion and AI-powered retrieval system built on a FastAPI backend with a ChromaDB vector store for semantic search, a Celery distributed task queue for asynchronous processing, and Redis as the message broker. The ingestion pipeline accepts content from multiple sources including URLs, uploaded documents, and raw text, then splits it into semantically meaningful chunks using recursive character splitting with overlap. Each chunk is embedded using a sentence transformer model and stored in ChromaDB with metadata including source URL, ingestion timestamp, content type, and hierarchical position. The semantic search API accepts natural language queries, embeds them with the same model, performs approximate nearest neighbor search in ChromaDB, and returns ranked results with relevance scores and source attribution. A content generation endpoint chains retrieved context with an LLM prompt to produce new content grounded in the ingested knowledge base, reducing hallucination by constraining generation to stored facts. The Celery worker pool handles long-running ingestion tasks asynchronously, reporting progress via Redis pub/sub. The frontend is deployed to Cloudflare Pages at ace-site.pages.dev and communicates with the FastAPI backend via REST endpoints. Background jobs include periodic re-embedding when the model is updated and dead-link detection for URL sources.

diagrams.name: openclaw-swarm-architecture
diagrams.description: OpenClaw Swarm is an autonomous multi-agent system consisting of 10 specialized AI agents coordinated through a central gateway running on port 18789. The gateway receives incoming requests via HTTP, classifies the task type and complexity, and routes work to one or more specialized agents including research, code generation, code review, analysis, summarization, planning, data processing, monitoring, testing, and orchestration agents. Each agent runs as an independent process with its own system prompt, tool access, and memory context, communicating via a shared message bus that supports both synchronous request-response and asynchronous publish-subscribe patterns. For complex multi-step tasks, the orchestration agent decomposes the request into a directed acyclic graph of subtasks, assigns each to the appropriate specialist agent, tracks completion status, and aggregates partial results into a final coherent response. Agents can collaborate by posting intermediate artifacts to the message bus, allowing downstream agents to build on upstream results. The Moltbook OpenPaw_PSM integration provides a persistent knowledge layer where agents can store and retrieve learned patterns, previous task outcomes, and domain-specific context. The gateway implements rate limiting, request queuing, and circuit breaker patterns to handle load spikes and agent failures gracefully. Health checks monitor each agent's responsiveness, automatically restarting unresponsive agents and redistributing their queued work.

diagrams.name: ruby-toolkit-architecture
diagrams.description: The Ruby Toolkit is a collection of 16 independent Ruby gem repositories that collectively provide 162 CLI commands for development workflow automation. Each gem is a standalone repository with its own gemspec, test suite, and GitHub Actions CI workflow that runs on push and pull request events. All gems depend on a shared core library that provides common utilities including configuration loading, colored terminal output, error handling, and plugin discovery. CLI entry points are installed as executable scripts symlinked into /opt/homebrew/bin/ so they are available system-wide without modifying PATH. The gems are organized by domain: file operations (bulk rename, deduplication, archival), git helpers (branch management, commit formatting, repository statistics), deployment automation (Cloudflare Pages, EAS builds, cPanel operations), system monitoring (disk usage, process management, network checks), and code generation scaffolds. A pre-commit hook framework shared across all 16 repos blocks commits containing credentials, API keys, or secrets by scanning staged files against a pattern list. Each repo enforces LF line endings via .gitattributes and uses frozen_string_literal pragmas. The symlink installation uses File.realpath to resolve the actual script location and sets BUNDLE_GEMFILE to the gem's own Gemfile, ensuring correct dependency resolution regardless of the current working directory.

diagrams.name: psm-brain-architecture
diagrams.description: PSM Brain is a session intelligence platform that captures, parses, and indexes Claude Code sessions for replay, search, and prompt template extraction, backed by a Neon Postgres serverless database. A session parser ingests raw Claude Code conversation logs, extracting structured data including user prompts, assistant responses, tool calls with arguments and outputs, token usage, timestamps, and session metadata such as working directory, project name, and duration. Parsed sessions are stored in normalized Postgres tables with full-text search indexes on prompt content and GIN indexes on JSONB metadata columns for efficient filtering by project, date range, tool usage, and outcome. The prompt library component identifies high-value prompt patterns from successful sessions, abstracting them into reusable templates with variable placeholders, categorized by task type (debugging, feature development, refactoring, deployment). A session replay viewer reconstructs the conversation timeline, showing each turn with syntax-highlighted code blocks, tool call visualizations, and timing information. The API layer exposes REST endpoints for session ingestion, search with faceted filtering, prompt template CRUD, and replay data retrieval. Analytics dashboards aggregate session data to surface metrics including sessions per day, average token usage, most-used tools, success rates by task type, and prompt effectiveness scores. The Neon Postgres connection uses connection pooling via the serverless driver to handle burst traffic without exhausting connection limits.

diagrams.name: cpanel-mcp-architecture
diagrams.description: cpanel-mcp is a Model Context Protocol server published to npm at version 0.1.1 that wraps cPanel UAPI and WHM API endpoints into 47 MCP tools accessible from Claude Code and other MCP-compatible clients. The server communicates with Claude Code via MCP stdio transport, receiving JSON-RPC tool invocation requests and returning structured responses. Internally, each tool maps to one or more cPanel UAPI or WHM API HTTP calls authenticated via API token, with the base URL and credentials loaded from environment variables or a configuration file stored at $VS/configs/credentials/cpanel/ with chmod 600 permissions. The 47 tools span seven domains: email management (create, delete, list accounts, set quotas, manage forwarders), DNS record management (add, delete, list records across zones), file operations (list, read, write, delete files on the hosting account), MySQL database administration (create and delete databases, manage users, set privileges), SSL certificate management (generate CSRs, install certificates, list existing certs), backup operations (create and list backups), and WHM server administration (create and manage hosting accounts, manage packages, monitor server load, restart services, check versions). The target hosting environment is a Namecheap shared hosting account running purplesquirrelmedia.io. Error handling normalizes cPanel's varied error response formats into consistent MCP error objects with human-readable messages. Request rate limiting prevents hitting cPanel's API throttle limits on shared hosting.
